title: Build a Hydrogen Atom
brief: Hydrogen is the simplest atom. It has 1 proton and 1 electron. Can you build it?
teacherNotes: Students should understand that hydrogen has 1 proton and 1 electron.

explanationQuiz.question: How many protons does a hydrogen atom have?

title: Make Water
brief: Water is made of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom. Connect them with bonds!
teacherNotes: Students connect 2 H and 1 O with single covalent bonds.

explanationQuiz.question: Why do hydrogen and oxygen stick together to make water?
options: They are pulled together by tiny magnets
Water is just one big atom

title: Make Methane
brief: Methane is a gas with 1 carbon and 4 hydrogen atoms. It is the main part of natural gas.
teacherNotes: Students build CH4 with 4 single bonds to carbon.

explanationQuiz.question: How many bonds does carbon make in methane?

title: Count the Particles
brief: A water molecule has 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom. Place exactly that many on the canvas to show the count, then press Check.
teacherNotes: Students count atoms in a pre-built water molecule.

explanationQuiz.question: How many atoms are in one water molecule?

title: Carbon Dioxide
brief: Carbon dioxide has 1 carbon and 2 oxygen atoms. The bonds are extra strong—can you make them?
teacherNotes: Students build CO2 with double bonds. Introduces double bonds conceptually.

explanationQuiz.question: What kind of bonds connect carbon and oxygen in CO₂?

title: Form Sodium Chloride
brief: Sodium and chlorine form an ionic bond. Sodium gives away an electron, and chlorine takes it. Build the salt crystal!
teacherNotes: Students learn about electron transfer and ionic bonding.

explanationQuiz.question: What happens to electrons when sodium and chlorine form salt?
options: Sodium gives an electron to chlorine
Chlorine gives an electron to sodium

title: Conserve Atoms
brief: In a chemical reaction, atoms are rearranged but never created or destroyed. Check that your reaction has the same atoms on both sides!
teacherNotes: Students run the water formation reaction and verify atom conservation.

explanationQuiz.question: What is true of every chemical reaction?
options: Atoms are rearranged but not created or destroyed
New atoms are made from energy

title: Ionic vs Covalent
brief: Some atoms share electrons. Others swap them. Can you tell which is which? Build both types of bonds!
teacherNotes: Students contrast covalent (water) and ionic (NaCl) bonding.

explanationQuiz.question: What is the main difference between ionic and covalent bonds?
options: Ionic bonds transfer electrons; covalent bonds share them
Covalent bonds only happen between metals

title: Make Ammonia
brief: Ammonia is NH₃. Nitrogen and hydrogen share electrons to fill their outer shells. Build it!
teacherNotes: Students build NH3, reinforcing valence electron concepts.

explanationQuiz.question: How many hydrogen atoms are bonded to nitrogen in ammonia?

title: The Triple Bond
brief: Nitrogen gas has one of the strongest bonds in nature—a triple bond! Can you build it?
teacherNotes: Students build N2 with a triple bond. Advanced covalent bonding.

explanationQuiz.question: How many pairs of electrons are shared in a triple bond?

title: Hydrogen Chloride
brief: Hydrogen and chlorine react to form hydrogen chloride gas. Build the reactants on the left and the products on the right!
teacherNotes: Students run the H2 + Cl2 -> 2HCl reaction and verify atom conservation.

explanationQuiz.question: When one H₂ and one Cl₂ react, how many HCl molecules form?

title: Methane Combustion
brief: When methane burns, it combines with oxygen to make carbon dioxide and water. Show this reaction and check that atoms are conserved!
teacherNotes: Students run CH4 + 2O2 -> CO2 + 2H2O, a classic combustion reaction.

explanationQuiz.question: What products form when methane burns in oxygen?

title: Ammonia Synthesis
brief: Nitrogen and hydrogen combine to make ammonia. This is how fertilizers are made! Build the reaction and conserve atoms.
teacherNotes: Students run N2 + 3H2 -> 2NH3, the Haber-Bosch process simplified.

explanationQuiz.question: How many H₂ molecules are needed to react with one N₂ to make ammonia?

title: Build a Helium Atom
brief: Helium fills birthday balloons because it is lighter than air. It has 2 protons and 2 electrons.
teacherNotes: Reinforce that the atomic number equals the proton count.

explanationQuiz.question: How many electrons does a neutral helium atom have?

title: Build an Oxygen Atom
brief: Oxygen is the gas you breathe. It has 8 protons. Build a neutral oxygen atom!
teacherNotes: Students build O with 8 protons and 8 electrons.

explanationQuiz.question: How many protons does an oxygen atom have?

title: Build a Carbon Atom
brief: Carbon is in pencils, diamonds and every living thing. Build a neutral carbon atom (6 protons, 6 electrons).
teacherNotes: Bridges atom-building toward later carbon-based molecules.

explanationQuiz.question: A neutral carbon atom has 6 protons. How many electrons does it have?

title: Hydrogen Gas
brief: Two hydrogen atoms can share one bond to become a hydrogen gas molecule (H₂). Try it!
teacherNotes: Simplest diatomic molecule — gentle intro to covalent bonding.

explanationQuiz.question: How many bonds connect the two hydrogens in H₂?

title: Oxygen Gas
brief: Oxygen in the air comes in pairs (O₂). The two atoms share a stronger double bond. Build it!
teacherNotes: Introduces the double bond before tackling CO₂.

explanationQuiz.question: What kind of bond holds the two oxygens together in O₂?

title: Build a Chlorine Atom
brief: Chlorine has 17 protons and is a reactive halogen. Build a neutral chlorine atom.
teacherNotes: Sets up the next mission on Cl₂ gas.

explanationQuiz.question: How many protons does a chlorine atom have?

title: Chlorine Gas
brief: Two chlorine atoms share one bond to form Cl₂, a yellow-green gas used to clean pools. Build it!
teacherNotes: Diatomic halogen with a single covalent bond.

explanationQuiz.question: What kind of bond is between the two chlorines in Cl₂?

title: Carbon Monoxide
brief: Carbon and oxygen can share a triple bond to form CO. It is invisible and dangerous, but interesting chemistry. Build the molecule!
teacherNotes: Reinforces triple bonds in a heteronuclear molecule.

explanationQuiz.question: How many bonds connect carbon and oxygen in carbon monoxide (CO)?

title: Hydrogen Peroxide
brief: Hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) is water with one extra oxygen. Two oxygens are bonded in the middle, with a hydrogen on each end. Build it!
teacherNotes: Introduces O–O single bonds; longer molecule chain than water.

explanationQuiz.question: How many oxygen atoms are in one molecule of hydrogen peroxide?

title: Split Water
brief: Pass electricity through water and it splits into hydrogen gas and oxygen gas. Show 2H₂O → 2H₂ + O₂.
teacherNotes: Reverse of water_formation. Demonstrates electrolysis and conservation.

explanationQuiz.question: What is needed to split water into hydrogen and oxygen gas?

title: Burn Carbon Monoxide
brief: Add oxygen to carbon monoxide and you get carbon dioxide. Build 2CO + O₂ → 2CO₂ on the canvas.
teacherNotes: Reactant CO has a triple bond; product CO₂ has two double bonds.

explanationQuiz.question: What does carbon monoxide turn into when it gets more oxygen?

title: Hydrogen Peroxide Decomposes
brief: Hydrogen peroxide breaks down into water and oxygen — that is the fizz when you pour it on a cut. Show 2H₂O₂ → 2H₂O + O₂.
teacherNotes: Decomposition reaction; two molecules in, two molecules out.

explanationQuiz.question: What does hydrogen peroxide break down into?

title: Methane Combustion Revisited
brief: Burning methane is what makes a gas stove flame. Re-run CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O and check that all 4 hydrogens and 4 oxygens are accounted for.
teacherNotes: Capstone review of multi-product combustion.

explanationQuiz.question: How many O₂ molecules are needed to burn one methane molecule?

title: Ammonia from Air
brief: Nitrogen from the air plus hydrogen makes ammonia, the start of most fertilizers. Show N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃ and balance the atoms.
teacherNotes: Reinforces the Haber-Bosch process and triple-bond cleavage.

explanationQuiz.question: Ammonia is mostly used to make what?
